slides.title: $_REQUEST – Combined GET and POST
slides.content:
# $_REQUEST

`$_REQUEST` is an associative array that contains the contents of `$_GET`, `$_POST`, and `$_COOKIE`. It's convenient but less secure because you don't know the source. Usually better to use `$_GET` or `$_POST` explicitly.

```php
$name = $_REQUEST['name'] ?? ''; // could be from GET or POST
```

slides.title: $_SERVER – Server and Execution Info
slides.content:
# $_SERVER

`$_SERVER` contains information about headers, paths, and script locations. Common entries:

*   `$_SERVER['PHP_SELF']` – filename of the currently executing script
*   `$_SERVER['REQUEST_METHOD']` – HTTP method (GET, POST, etc.)
*   `$_SERVER['REMOTE_ADDR']` – IP address of the user
*   `$_SERVER['HTTP_USER_AGENT']` – browser information
*   `$_SERVER['SERVER_NAME']` – server hostname

```php
if ($_SERVER['REQUEST_METHOD'] === 'POST') {
    // handle POST
}
```

slides.title: Using $_SERVER
slides.codeSnippet:
<?php
echo "You are visiting: " . $_SERVER['PHP_SELF'];
echo "Your IP: " . $_SERVER['REMOTE_ADDR'];
echo "Browser: " . $_SERVER['HTTP_USER_AGENT'];
?>

quizData.question: Which superglobal tells you the HTTP method used?
